---
bibliography: refs.bib
---

## Research Process
The research process followed a structured but iterative sequence, from initial scoping and literature review through survey design, data collection, analysis, and reporting (@fig-rp-flowchart). Each stage informed the next, with the analytical pipeline developed in parallel with data collection so that processing and estimation could begin as soon as fieldwork concluded.

The process was not strictly linear. When the intended vessel-based survey could not be realised within the field-season window, the design phase was revisited mid-project and the survey re-specified as a land-based, point-transect design (see [Design Revision](#revision)). This feedback loop is represented in the flowchart and reflects adaptive management of project risk rather than a departure from the planned process.

![Flowchart of the research process, from initial planning and survey design through data collection, analysis, and reporting of results.](/misc/research_process_flowchart.png){height=70% #fig-rp-flowchart}

A detailed Gantt chart (@fig-gantt) was developed at the proposal stage to schedule work packages against the fixed submission timeline. The schedule was deliberately layered, with literature review, methods development, and analysis preparation running in parallel with the field season rather than sequentially after it. This overlap provided the slack that absorbed the delay to vessel availability, so that the loss of vessel-based sampling did not propagate into a delay in delivery.

![Detailed project Gantt chart, illustrating the layering and parallel scheduling of tasks across the project phases.](/misc/gannt_chart.png){#fig-gantt}

## Distance Sampling
Distance sampling is a widely established and rigorously validated methodology for the systematic estimation of animal abundance and distribution [@bucklandDistanceSamplingMethods2015; @bucklandIntroductionDistanceSampling2001]. The method is based on the principle that the probability of detecting an animal decreases with increasing distance from the observer, by modelling this detection probability, approximately unbiased estimates of population density can be derived under the method's assumptions [@bucklandDistanceSamplingMethods2015; @bucklandIntroductionDistanceSampling2001; @thomasDistanceSoftwareDesign2010]. Since its formalisation, distance sampling has been successfully adapted across a broad range of terrestrial, aerial, and aquatic survey contexts, demonstrating considerable methodological flexibility [@dawsonDesignFieldMethods2008; @dickUsingDistanceSampling2011; @bucklandDistanceSamplingMethods2015].

Within the field of marine mammal science, distance sampling has become one of the most frequently applied methods for estimating cetacean abundance and distribution [@dickUsingDistanceSampling2011; @gnoneCetaceansMediterraneanSea2023; @raudinoAerialAbundanceEstimates2023], forming the basis of large-scale systematic surveys conducted by organisations including the `#gls("iwc")`{=typst}, `#gls("accobams")`{=typst} and `#gls("noaa")`{=typst} [@AbundanceCetaceansCalifornia; @macleodAbundanceAtlanticWhitesided2004; @stephanisSummerSpatialDistribution2008]. Shipboard line transect surveys, in particular, have been employed extensively to monitor cetacean populations across a range of geographic scales, from oceanic basins to coastal and riverine habitats [@AbundanceCetaceansCalifornia; @lerczakCalculatingSightingDistances1998; @dawsonDesignFieldMethods2008; @dickUsingDistanceSampling2011; @gnoneCetaceansMediterraneanSea2023; @thomasDistanceSoftwareDesign2010]. 


## Survey design
The study was originally designed as a vessel-based line-transect survey — the first dedicated systematic cetacean survey within the `#gls("bgtw")`{=typst} — following established distance-sampling methodology [@bucklandIntroductionDistanceSampling2001; @thomasDesigningLineTransect2023]. Although logistical constraints ultimately required a shift to land-based sampling, the strata defined during this design were retained for the land-based survey, and the proposed design is therefore described here.

To meet the basic assumptions for a good survey design mentioned by Thomas et al. [-@thomasDesigningLineTransect2023] the starting point of the survey was chosen randomly using a random seed for every transect design within the `dssd` package [@marshallDssdDistanceSampling2025]. To minimise the time travelling between survey legs (off-effort time) an equal spaced zigzag design was chosen. The zigzag design was laid out so there are only a minimal number of apexes, to avoid biases caused by responsive movement of the species [@dawsonDesignFieldMethods2008].

To prevent the survey legs to spread, due to the shape of the survey area [@strindbergZigzagSurveyDesigns2004; @dawsonSmallboatSurveysCoastal2004; @dawsonDesignFieldMethods2008] the area was split into three strata (East, South and West, see @fig-area). Within those strata the zigzag design followed the longest axis of the area and as perpendicular to the coastline as possible [@strindbergZigzagSurveyDesigns2004; @dawsonDesignFieldMethods2008; @thomasDesigningLineTransect2023; @strindbergZigzagSurveyDesigns2004; @dawsonDesignFieldMethods2008]. The survey vessel followed this designed survey as closely as possible, while maintaining a speed of 10 knots to ensure even coverage and effort [@dawsonDesignFieldMethods2008]. A Garmin 78s GPS Device was used to check the current location of the vessel as well as record the track and locations of the sightings.

![Sampling area divided in the three strata (East, South and West) in order to achieve effective transects. Zigzag transects are overlayed (orange) and follow the longest axis of the strata. The starting point of the transects will be shifted every survey.](/figures/region_transects.png){#fig-area}

In order to generate reproducible and standardised results, the observers followed the survey guidelines (see @app-protocol) for the survey protocol and data collection methods (for checklist, see @app-checklist). The survey was conducted in accordance with the ethical guidelines for marine mammal research, ensuring minimal disturbance to the animals and their habitats.


By comparing systematic outputs against existing opportunistic datasets (OBIS, https://mapper.obis.org/), this study critically evaluated the relative value of each monitoring approach for informing conservation and management decisions in geographically bounded marine territories.



## Photo Identification (Photo-ID)
Alongside the systematic distance-sampling data, opportunistic photo identification data were collected, to further assess species composition and distribution within the `#gls("bgtw")`{=typst}. Small cetaceans, such as short-beaked common dolphins (*Delphinus delphis*), striped dolphins (*Stenella coeruleoalba*) and bottlenose dolphins (*Tursiops truncatus*) are the most commonly encountered species in the `#gls("bgtw")`{=typst} [@canales-caceresDistributionBehaviourStriped2023; @gnoneCetaceansMediterraneanSea2023; @olaya-ponzonePhotoIdentificationCatalogueDelphinus2025; @olaya-ponzoneMixedspeciesDolphinGroups2025; @olaya-ponzoneSpatioTemporalPatternsLimited2026; @olaya-ponzoneVesselInducedBehavioural2025]. 
Because of the expected species in this region, the method to capture `#gls("pid")`{=typst} was therefore adapted and applied to small cetaceans [@wuersigMethodsPhotoidentificationSmall1990; @roselPhotoidentificationCapturemarkrecaptureTechniques2011].

Images were captured using a Canon EOS R50 with a Canon 100–400 mm telephoto lens, following a protocol (@app-protocol) modified to incorporate `#gls("pid")`{=typst} best practice [@roselPhotoidentificationCapturemarkrecaptureTechniques2011; @karczmarskiIndividualIdentificationPhotographic2022]. Photo-ID records were organised, filtered, ranked, and matched across encounters using the DISCOVERY software [@chanApplicationMultispeciesPhotoID2022; @chanIndoPacificHumpbackDolphins2017; @chanUnknownComponentWellknown2022], which supports the creation and maintenance of individual identification catalogues. Photo-ID outputs are provided as supplementary material (@app-photoid).

## Design revision{#revision .unnumbered}
The study was originally designed and proposed as a vessel-based line-transect survey . However, the intended university research vessel could not be launched within the field-season window, as arrangements for its transport and craning into the marina were not completed in time. Despite the university's repeated assertions, both in the project planning and proposal phase and in subsequent updates, that the vessel would be made available, this has not been the case. In consultation with the project supervisor, continued delay was judged unfeasible given the fixed timeframe, and the survey was consequently revised to a land-based station design covering the same three strata.

This revision has two consequences for interpretation. First, the land-based stations sampled only part of the `#gls("bgtw")`{=typst}, so density and distribution estimates represent the surveyed sectors rather than the territory as a whole. Second, the change from randomly placed transects to fixed observation points alters the sampling geometry from line- to point-transect distance sampling, with implications addressed in the Discussion (see [limitations](#limits)).


## Land-based Survey
Following the logistical constraints described above, land-based cetacean surveys represent a viable and cost-effective alternative to the more expensive vessel-based surveys. The utilisation of vantage points facilitates a more profound interpretation of the observed behaviour. The behaviour state was defined using an adaptation of Neumann et al. [-@neumannBehaviourEcologyCommon] and Hawkins and Gartside [-@hawkinsInteractiveBehavioursBottlenose2009] (@tbl-behave). In the context of behavioural studies, land-based surveys have been a particularly effective method of data collection, as they do not influence the behaviour of cetaceans or introduce bias via the survey vessel [@mannBehavioralSamplingMethods1999].


| **Behaviour State** | **Description** |
|:-- |:---------- |
| Travelling | Dolphins move in one defined direction and surface consistently. |
| Milling | Dolphins change swimming direction frequently but remain in the same general area. |
| Feeding/Foraging | Dolphins actively pursue prey or capture fish. Associated with deep diving, fast swimming; frequent direction changes and inconsistent breath-intervals. |
| Socialising | At least two dolphins make physical contact at the surface. Like chasing each other, body contact, petting or copulation. Often accompanied by breaching or other surface activities. |
| Resting | Dolphins stay close to the surface and to each other. They regularly surface at regular intervals and move only very slowly or do not move forward at all. |

: Definition table of behaviour states of dolphins adapted from Neumann (2005) and Hawkins and Gartside (2009). {#tbl-behave}


Surveys were conducted from three vantage points selected to cover the majority of the `#gls("bgtw")`{=typst}: Europa Point, Prince George, and Sandy Bay (@tbl-landspots, @fig-spots). Observers remained on-effort for 10-minute periods separated by 20-minute breaks, allowing observer rest and reducing double-counting by permitting animals to leave the surveyed area [@gutierrez-munozPatternsTrendsCetacean2021]. In total, 174 surveys (including pilot surveys) were conducted; surveys were postponed when visibility was poor or sea state exceeded Beaufort 5.


| **Station** | **Sector** | **Latitude** | **Longitude** | **Height (m a.s.l.)** | **Scan sector** |
|:---------|:----|:------:|:------:|:------:|:------|
| Prince George | West | 36°06′50.40″ N | 5°20′58.51″ W | 82 | 207–335° |
| Europa Point | South | 36°06′33.48″ N | 5°20′44.34″ W | 18 | 060–220° |
| Sandy Bay | East | 36°07′47.99″ N | 5°20′31.65″ W | 82 | 016–155° |

: Land-based observation stations, with sector surveyed, position, height above sea level, and the compass sector scanned from each. {#tbl-landspots}


![Point of views of the three selected vantage points (Prince George (A), Sandy Bay (B) and Europa Point (C), facing left (a), right (b) and a wide shot of the spot (c). Overview of the area covered by each spots sector (D).](/figures/spots_comb.png){#fig-spots}

Observations were carried out both with the naked eye and using binoculars (Fujinon 7 × 50) using the continuous horizon scanning method [@mannBehavioralSamplingMethods1999]. When an observation was made, the time, number of reticles, horizontal bearing, species and group size were documented; the presence of birds or fish was also noted, where possible. Photographs were recorded opportunistically using a Canon EOS R50 camera  with a telephoto lens (Canon 100 – 400 mm) to support subsequent species confirmation and behavioural assessments.



## Data Cleaning
To streamline data handling and ensure reproducibility, a data-cleaning pipeline was implemented in R [@zotero-item-2907]. The pipeline automatically imported the field data sheets and applied a set of quality-control checks, validation rules that halt execution when a record violates a predefined criterion, prior to any downstream processing. Custom functions then derived sighting distances from the recorded reticle readings and bearings (see [Distance calculation](#calc-dis), @app-disR), yielding a cleaned dataset for subsequent analysis.


## Data Analysis
### Calculating distance {#calc-dis .unnumbered}
To calculate the distance of the sighting ($d$), the number of reticles ($r$) from the horizon line or, in the case of the western side, the coastline, was recorded. Utilising the observers' height ($h$), the reticle factor ($F$) and the provided formula, the distance can be calculated [@bucklandIntroductionDistanceSampling2001]. Implementing the Earth's radius ($R$) was used to make the formula more accurate [@lerczakCalculatingSightingDistances1998]:

$$
d=\frac{h}{\tan{\left(r\times F+\sqrt{\frac{2\times h}{R}}\right)}}
$$

Using the distance and the bearing a circle can be projected from the observers' position and the location can be calculated and plotted (@app-incl).

Because of the enclosed nature of the western survey area (the Bay of Gibraltar), the far shore of the bay rather than the sea horizon was visible along most bearings from the Prince George station. As the reticle method derives distance from the angular separation between a sighting and a known reference line, the standard horizon-based formula could not be applied here: the coastline sits closer than the true horizon and therefore at a different (greater) angle of depression, so treating it as the horizon would overestimate sighting distances.

For this station the reference depression angle was derived from the distance to the coastline along each sighting's bearing instead. Using a detailed polygon of the bay's landmasses (including the detached mole off Algeciras, which forms the first visible obstacle on some bearings), a ray was projected from the observation point along the recorded horizontal bearing, and the distance to the first intersection with land was taken as the reference distance ($d_{ref}$). The depression angle of the reference line was then calculated as:

$$
\theta_{ref}=\frac{h}{d_{ref}}+\frac{d_{ref}}{2R}
$$

where $h$ is the observer height and $R$ the Earth's radius, with the second term correcting for the curvature of the sea surface between observer and coastline. This replaces the horizon dip term ($\sqrt{\frac{2\times h}{R}}$) used at the open-water stations; the two expressions are equivalent when $d_{ref}$ equals the horizon distance. Bearings that pointed out of the bay mouth, where no coastline was intersected, reverted to the standard horizon reference. The sighting distance was then obtained from the total depression angle ($\theta_{ref}+reticleAngle$) as before (see @app-disR or [GitHub](https://github.com/datsimon-design/Masters_Project) for examples in R).

### Statistical Analysis
All statistical analyses were carried out in R [@zotero-item-2907]. `#gls("spue")`{=typst}, calculated as the number of sightings per on-effort scan, was strongly right-skewed and zero-inflated and did not meet the assumptions of parametric analysis of variance (normality of residuals and homogeneity of variance); a non-parametric approach was therefore adopted, with each on-effort scan treated as the sampling unit. Differences in `#gls("spue")`{=typst} among the three active observation stations (Europa, Sandy Bay and Prince George) were tested using a Kruskal–Wallis rank-sum test [@KruskalWallis1952]. Where the omnibus test was significant, pairwise differences were identified using Dunn's post-hoc test [@dunnMultipleComparisonsUsing1964], which operates on the pooled ranks of the Kruskal–Wallis test and accounts appropriately for tied values. P-values were adjusted for multiple comparisons using the Benjamini–Hochberg false discovery rate procedure [@benjaminiControllingFalseDiscovery1995].

### Detection Function {#det-func .unnumbered}
The `Distance` package requires a flatfile format [@bucklandDistanceSamplingMethods2015] (@app-flat); the data from the effort and sighting logs were therefore first mutated and then joined, the effort was calculated by dividing the sector degrees by 360. The area for each wedge was calculated and added to the data frame. The truncation distance was determined separately for each sampling location based on the corresponding distribution of observed distances (see [Detection model fitting](#model_fit) for values). Detection functions were first fitted per station using the `ds` function, following a two-step procedure: candidate key functions (half-normal and hazard-rate) were compared by `#gls("aic")`{=typst}, goodness-of-fit test (`gof_ds`), and Q–Q plot, and the best-supported key function was then tested with candidate covariates (wave height, glare, sea state, and wind speed) to assess whether they improved fit. 

Because the per-station samples were insufficient to support stable detection functions [@bucklandDistanceSamplingMethods2015], a single pooled detection function was additionally fitted across all stations, following the same two-step procedure; for the pooled model, observation height was tested as an additional covariate alongside those above. The pooled detection function was used for the density estimates reported here, with per-station estimates retained for comparison. Density was estimated separately for groups and individuals.


### Kernel Density Estimation (KDE)
`#gls("kde")`{=typst} is a well-established tool for describing the spatial distribution and relative abundance of cetaceans [@raymentKernelDensityEstimates2009; @milmannTemporalSpatialDistribution2017]. It was used here to describe the relative spatial distribution of cetaceans within the BGTW. The locations of all on-effort sightings—derived from reticle-based radial distances and compass bearings (see [Calculation](#calc-dis)), were projected to UTM zone 30N so that smoothing was performed in metric units, and used as input to the kernel density estimation. A bivariate kernel density surface was computed in R using the `ks` package [@duongKsKernelSmoothing2005], with each sighting weighted by its group size so that the surface reflected the relative density of individuals rather than of sightings alone. The smoothing bandwidth was selected automatically using a plug-in bandwidth matrix [@duongPluginBandwidthMatrices2003], and density was evaluated over a 100 × 100 grid spanning the survey area. KDE is a well-established tool for describing the spatial distribution and relative abundance of cetaceans [@canales-caceresDistributionBehaviourStriped2023a; @raymentKernelDensityEstimates2009; @milmannTemporalSpatialDistribution2017]. 

Because the resulting surface is a normalised probability density (integrating to one), it provides a descriptive, relative measure of spatial concentration; it was not corrected for observation effort or imperfect detection, which were instead accounted for separately through the distance-sampling density estimation ([Detection Function](#det-func)). The surface was visualised over the study area and overlaid with the observation stations and individual sightings to identify areas of elevated cetacean occurrence.

### Comparison with opportunistic records
To evaluate the systematic survey against existing opportunistic data, occurrence records of *Delphinus delphis* were retrieved from the `#gls("obis")`{=typst} ([OBIS Mapper](https://mapper.obis.org/)) using the `robis` package [@provoostRobisOceanBiodiversity2022], constrained to a bounding box encompassing the study area (-5.438/-5.248; 36.05/36.195). Records lacking coordinates were excluded, and the remainder were projected to CRS 4326 and assigned to survey sectors by spatial intersection with the stratum polygons. The spatial distribution of opportunistic records was then compared with that of the systematic on-effort sightings, both visually (@fig-obis) and by tabulating record counts per sector (@tbl-obis_comp). Because opportunistic records carry no associated survey effort, the comparison was restricted to spatial coverage and was not interpreted in terms of relative abundance.

This study was carried out under the University of Gibraltar approval, Ethics ID: 259/2026/UniGib
